info.description: This problem set focuses on fungal pathogens and antifungal agents. The difficulty may vary a lot through out the set, since this set contains a mix of four different cognitive levels of complexity (recall, understand, apply, analyze). The questions are already reviewed, so mistakes should have already been minimized, but if you come across one, you can report through the Forms in the "Info and Links".

q1.prompt: A specimen from a patient with a suspected pulmonary infection demonstrates long, branching filaments that stain positively with a 1% sulfuric acid decolorizer. Which of the following stains is specifically used to highlight the waxy, lipid-rich cell wall of this organism?

q2.prompt: A 45-year-old male undergoing chemotherapy for acute myeloid leukemia develops a high fever, pleuritic chest pain, and hemoptysis despite being on broad-spectrum antibiotics. A CT scan of the chest reveals cavitary lesions with a 'halo sign.' A serum assay for a specific polysaccharide component of the fungal cell wall is positive. Which of the following is the target of this diagnostic test?

q3.prompt: During a microbiology lecture, a professor describes the asexual reproductive cycle of certain filamentous fungi. Which of the following structures is seen as barrel-shaped cells resulting from the fragmentation of septate hyphae?

q4.prompt: A bone marrow transplant recipient who was receiving voriconazole prophylaxis develops a sudden fever and wedge-shaped pulmonary infiltrates. A lung biopsy demonstrates irregular, broad, non-septate hyphae branching at wide angles. Why was the initial prophylactic agent likely ineffective?

options.label: The agent cannot penetrate the fungal cell wall
The agent inhibits mitosis rather than membrane synthesis
The agent lacks activity against the causative agents
The organism is a fungus-like bacterium resistant to azoles
The patient has a specific B-cell defect preventing drug activation

q5.prompt: A patient is prescribed terbinafine for the treatment of a fungal infection. Which of the following describes the primary mechanism of action for this medication?

options.label: Binding to ergosterol to form membrane pores
Inhibition of the fungal enzyme squalene epoxidase
Interference with microtubule function during mitosis

q6.prompt: An HIV-positive patient with a CD4+ T-cell count of 150 cells/mm^3 presents with a nonproductive cough, severe hypoxia, and diffuse ground-glass opacities on a chest X-ray. Which of the following stains is best used to visualize the cup-shaped cysts of the most likely causative agent in bronchoalveolar lavage fluid?

q7.prompt: A 50-year-old male agricultural worker presents with a five-year history of a slow-growing, painless mass on his left foot. Physical examination reveals a firm, swollen lesion with multiple sinus tracts that discharge fluid containing small, hard black grains. Microscopic analysis of these grains confirms the diagnosis of eumycetoma caused by *Madurella mycetomatis*. Which of the following microscopic morphologies is most characteristic of this pathogen in clinical specimens?

q8.prompt: A medical student is reviewing the morphological classification of fungi and encounters a species described as an encapsulated yeast capable of causing human infection. Which fungus is being described?

q9.prompt: A patient with invasive candidiasis is treated with an echinocandin such as caspofungin. How does this class of medication specifically cause fungal cell death?

options.label: Binding to tubulin to disrupt the mitotic spindle
Forming pores in the cell membrane by binding sterols
Inhibiting 1,3-β-D-glucan synthase to weaken the cell wall
Inhibiting the conversion of squalene to squalene epoxide

q10.prompt: A 7-year-old child presents with a scaly, itchy patch of hair loss on the scalp. The parents note that the child recently began playing with a stray kitten found in the neighborhood. Which of the following fungal genera is most likely responsible for this infection?
